title: The Talents Entrusted
subtitle: The Lord, like a man going abroad, hands His servants His goods and departs — and returns to settle accounts

stillness: Be watchful, the Lord says, for you do not know the day.
Sit still, and consider only this: what have you received?
Let the soul grow quiet, and reckon what it owes.
gospel: The Bridegroom, like a man setting out on a journey,
called his servants and handed them his goods:
to one five talents, to another two, to another one,
to each according to his own ability,
and at once he set out.
He who had received five went and worked with them,
and gained another five.
Likewise he who had received two gained another two.
But he who had received the one went and dug in the earth,
and hid his lord's money.
After a long time the lord of those servants came,
and settled accounts with them.
He who had received five came and offered another five,
and he who had received two offered another two.
And his lord said to each of them:
'Well done, good and faithful servant.
Because you were faithful over a few things,
I will set you over many.
Enter into the joy of your lord.'
Then he also came who had received the one talent, and said:
'Lord, I knew that you are a hard man,
reaping where you did not sow,
and gathering where you did not scatter;
and in fear I went and hid your talent in the earth.
Behold, here you have what is yours.'
His lord answered him: 'Wicked servant, and slothful,
you knew that I reap where I did not sow,
and gather where I did not scatter.'
behold: Behold the Bridegroom, like a man journeying far,
carrying our flesh to the heavenly homeland —
for heaven was a pilgrimage to the flesh,
and He is not seen by us in this present life.
See Him call His servants and hand them His goods:
to one five talents, to another two, to another one,
to each according to his own capacity,
that He might burden no one, and fail no one;
and from this learn that God commands nothing impossible.
See the first go off in the affection of his will
and work with the gift of his five senses,
eternal things from things of time.
See the third go off and dig in the earth —
in the bend of an earthly intention
and a fleshly way of acting —
and hide his lord's money in the ground,
with no building up of his neighbor.
consider: In these three servants are signified every kind of man:
the imperfect, the perfect, and the wicked.
They double the talents who make good works from them;
they hide them who live idly,
or who from them seek the gains of this world.
To bury the talent in the earth, says Gregory,
is to bind a received gift to earthly business,
and to seek no spiritual gain.
The lord came after a long time —
that is, at the second coming —
and demanded a reckoning from each,
exacting with interest what he had handed over.
Consider what each man received,
and weigh what gain he brings back from it.
'Well done' — it is the cry of one rejoicing:
good, by likeness to God;
servant, by humility in yourself;
faithful, by usefulness to your neighbor.
Few are the things given us in this world,
many are the things kept for us in that one.
This fear of the slothful servant
is nothing but a faint-heartedness in doing good.
Though he did not lose the talent,
yet he is guilty of the loss —
like one who, given seed to sow,
does not sow it when sowing-time has come.
'I judge those to whom I did not preach,' the Lord seems to say;
'how much more you, to whom I entrusted the talent of my knowledge.'
prayer: who in mercy have handed over to us your gifts,
and warn us, watchful and diligent,
to bring forth fruit from them:
to trade watchfully with the gifts I have received from you,
and to multiply them by the fruit of good works;
and so to use them well —
for your glory, my neighbor's building up,
that I may deserve to be rewarded with your good and faithful servants,
and, at your bidding, to enter the joy of eternal happiness,
and to receive what you have prepared for those who love you,

latinJewel.latin: Pauca sunt quæ in hoc seculo nobis præstantur, multa quæ in illo servantur.
latinJewel.english: Few are the things bestowed on us in this world; many are the things kept for us in that one.
